system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 3: Homonyms

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 3 — Homonyms

_Pages 12-14._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Root-based chord memorization

> 3. Remember the chord by where the root is; this way assumes that: a) there is a root in the chord somewhere,
> b)you are able to locate it; and c) you know the names of the notes on the fingerboard. For instance, suppose you
> have never learned this chord:

**Significance (p. 12).** This defines the third technique for memorizing chord forms — anchoring on the root — and states its three prerequisites explicitly.

## Root anchor technique practice

> The root in this chord is on the 4th string, so if you know the names
> of your notes on the 4th string, you will be able to transpose it and memorize it fairly
>
> 7 easily by focusing on the root as a visual anchor point, This technique takes practice
>
> U but it works very well, especially as a last resort when the other techniques don't seem
>
> to be doing the job.

**Significance (p. 12).** This explains how the root-anchor technique works in practice and establishes when to deploy it as a rule.

## Fingerboard note-name drills

> To learn this, you might wish to make up drills like: ‘‘Find the A notes on each string;
> likewise with B, C, D, etc.; then Ap, Bd, etc., then A#, B#, etc.” or any other drill you can think up that seems
> effective; or you can work on your note reading - this too will eventually teach you the note names on the finger-
> board

**Significance (p. 12).** This provides concrete drill examples for learning note names across all strings, a prerequisite skill for the root-anchor memorization technique.

## Combining memorization methods

> Most players use a combination of all three methods outlined here, so experiment with all of them and you will
> find what works best for you.

**Significance (p. 12).** This gives the reader a practical rule-of-thumb: eclecticism across all three techniques is the norm among experienced players.

## Definition of chord homonyms

> Webster’s dictionary defines the word homonym as “a word with the same pronunciation as another but with a
> different meaning.” In other words, the two words sound the same but come from different places (like read and reed
> ot principal and principle). In playing through this book, you are going to encounter chord homonyms, that is, chords
> that sound the same but have different origins and names.

**Significance (p. 13).** This formally defines the central concept of the chapter by analogy to linguistic homonyms, establishing the terminological framework for the rest of the discussion.

## Homonym naming rule: context decides

> So, as you can see, the naming of homonyms will be determined by “what is easiest” or Just plain common sense.

**Significance (p. 13).** This states the governing rule for resolving homonym naming ambiguity: harmonic context and common sense dictate which name applies.

## Homonym naming: Em6 example

> If you are working with the progression, say, Em Am, and you see this chord being used in place of Em, guess
> what it’s going to be called (how about Em6?),

**Significance (p. 13).** This is the first of three concrete examples showing how harmonic context determines which name a homonym chord receives.

## Homonym naming: C#m7b5 example

> Likewise, if you are working with C#m7 F#7 Bm and you run into this chord being used in place of C#m7, you
> can expect it to be named C#m7b5.

**Significance (p. 13).** This second homonym example demonstrates that the same chord form takes a minor-seventh-flat-five name when functioning in a minor-key context.

## Homonym naming: A9 example

> Finally if you are working with Em7 A7 D and you find this chord being used in place of A7, then it will be called
> AQ.

**Significance (p. 13).** This third example completes the set, showing the same chord form named as a dominant ninth when substituting for a V7 chord.

## Knowing all chord-form names

> You will
> have to know all the useful names for a chord form or you will just be making it hard for yourself in the long run.

**Significance (p. 13).** This is a direct rule requiring the student to learn every valid name for each chord form as a practical necessity.

## Definition of diatonic

> These chords are said to be DIATONIC to the key of C (in this book, diatonic means: using only
> notes of the scale)

**Significance (p. 13).** This gives the book's working definition of 'diatonic,' a foundational term used throughout the chord progressions material.

## Chord analysis defined

> Analyze, as used here, means to find out what tones of the chord are present and accounted for (many chord
> forms leave out Sths or roots or even other tones sometimes).

**Significance (p. 14).** This defines the analytical method the student must apply to chord diagrams throughout the book.

## Chords built in 3rd intervals

> CHORDS BY 3rd INTERVALS
>
> + Triads — 13 $5 liths —135
> ; Ith —1357 13ths —~ 135
> } Oths ~ 13579

**Significance (p. 14).** This table encodes the fundamental rule that chords are constructed by stacking thirds and lists expected tones for each chord type, serving as a reference for chord analysis.

## Voice leading definition

> VOICE LEADING (voice leading is the art of connecting each note of a given chord with each note in a following
> f chord. See CHORD CHEMISTRY ~ Section 13.)

**Significance (p. 14).** This defines voice leading as the principle behind why chords with missing tones are chosen, linking chord construction choices to an advanced harmonic concept.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
